data.title: Enbridge: I'm Buying Following This Quarter
data.description:
Introduction

I’ve been a longtime bull on Enbridge (ENB) as I believe that it is one of the companies operating in the midstream sector that has one of the best moats. Since I last reviewed it, ENB has been on a tear, returning 11.19% in stock appreciation in a little bit over 2 months. With the company just releasing earnings and showcasing that it is reinforcing its position as a North American energy infrastructure leader, I believe that this is the right time to showcase why Enbridge is a buy.

Current Dynamics

Q4 has been a landmark period for Enbridge as it showcased significant earnings growth and operational excellence. The company reported GAAP EPS of C$0.89 a share, which is a stark contrast Y/Y, as it was standing at C$0.23 per share. The GAAP figures were boosted by non-cash, unrealized changes in the fair value of derivative instruments; the underlying adjusted metrics tell a story of sustained strength. Adjusted earnings grew by 17% on a Y/Y basis, and estimates were standing at around 77 cents a share, while it came in at C$0.88, which is highly impressive. The beat was largely thanks to higher asset utilization across the liquids and gas transmission networks, as well as the immediate accretive impact of the acquired gas utility business.

Distributable cash flow rose to C$3.21B, up from C$3.07B Y/Y, and supports the company’s goal of maintaining a dividend payout ratio of between 60% and 70% of DCF. Thus, the company increased the quarterly dividend by 3% to reach $3.88 annualized, signifying management’s confidence in the visibility of future cash flows. Plus, this marks the 31st consecutive year of dividend growth.

Diving deeper into ENB’s business segment, the liquid pipelines continue to be the cornerstone of the portfolio, as it generates high cash flows through the irreplaceable Mainline System and associated feeder networks. Adj. EBITDA increased by C$51MM in the quarter compared to the same period in 2024. This was the result of higher demand on the Mainline System and the implementation of annual escalators and surcharges. The Mainline averaged a throughput of 3.1MMbpd through 2025 which is a testament to the system’s strategic importance, plus it was in apportionment for 9 months of the year, telling us that shipper demand consistently exceeded available capacity. This has persisted despite the start of the Trans Mountain Expansion, which management had already factored into long-term financial projections.

The gas transmission segment has also emerged as a primary growth engine, particularly as the demand for natural gas to power data centers and LNG export facilities accelerates. The all of the above approach is perhaps most visible here, where natural gas is positioned as the essential baseload partner for renewable energy. ENB is advancing over 50 data center opportunities across NA, which would require as much as 10 Bcf/d of new takeaway capacity.

On the gas distribution and storage front, since the acquisition of three premier US natural gas utilities from Dominion Energy (D), the utility weight has increased significantly in ENB’s earnings. The segment now serves over 7.1MM customers and is the largest natural gas utility franchise in North America. A critical point of my bullish stance on Enbridge has been the utility segment and its positive regulatory outcomes. See, for example, Enbridge Gas North Carolina reached an interim settlement with an allowed ROE of 9.65% and a $34MM revenue requirement increase. In Utah, a new revenue requirement increase of $62MM became effective at the start of the year. These settlements support quick-cycle capital recovery mechanisms, allowing ENB to reinvest in the safety and reliability of its distribution networks with a predictable return.

Based on this, the company reaffirmed its 2023 to 2026 near-term growth of 7 to 9% for Adj. EBITDA and 4 to 6% for adj. EPS and approximately 3% for DCF per share. Furthermore, the company is expected to see growth accelerate past 2026 to around 5% annually.

Valuation

Enbridge has generally been more expensive than its peers thanks to its Mainline moat and its entrenched position in the Canadian energy landscape. It currently has a P/E Non-GAAP of 25.07, the second highest in the peer group, below Williams (WMB) at 33.87. ENB also has an EV/EBITDA of 16.01, which is at the higher end of the range. And though it has a high FCF generation, the company only has an FCF yield of 2.1% due to the heightened share price. ENB still has a great yield at these prices, as it currently stands at 5.2% on a USD basis.

Looking at the growth prospects, ENB currently has a record-high secured capital backlog of $39B that is diversified across all four business segments, providing a clear runway for growth through the end of the decade. The "all of the above" strategy ensures that ENB is not betting on a single energy outcome but is instead positioned to benefit from the general increase in energy demand, independent of the source. Furthermore, the company has a high degree of protection against inflationary pressures, as over 80% of the company’s EBITDA has built-in inflation protection, either through contractual escalators in its LT agreements or through regulatory mechanisms in its utility business. Plus, the company has a great commercial structure that is incredibly robust, with over 98% of EBITDA derived from regulated or take-or-pay frameworks.

I’m also enthusiastic about the prospects of the company in renewable energy, as it has over 750 MW of renewable power generation under construction, specifically to support Meta’s data center operations. Other projects in the pipeline include the Sequoia solar farm and the Clear Fork Solar project.

Risks

Enbridge still has a significant debt burden, with a current debt-to-EBITDA ratio reported at 4.8x, which is currently at the top of ENB's target range. This means that the company is particularly sensitive to interest rate fluctuations, as higher rates increase financing costs and can pressure DCF down the line

Furthermore, as the company has become so massive, it may become increasingly hard to maintain high per-share growth rates over the long term. As the valuation metrics are higher than peers' average, this could lead to share volatility if growth targets are not met.

Conclusion

Following the quarterly release, I can say that Enbridge is at the height of its operational powers. All of the mentioned tailwinds for shareholders point to the management successfully executing on a long-term strategic vision. I believe that ENB is well-positioned to continue its multi-decade track record of creating shareholder value. I upgrade my rating to a Strong Buy.

data.title: Private Real Estate Debt: A Banner 2025, With More Room To Run
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

Introduction

Total loan originations increased 47% year-to-date through 3Q 2025, reaching their fifth-highest level since 1Q 2002. Outstanding mortgage debt rose to nearly $4.9 trillion as of 2Q 2025, up 4.5% year-over-year. Despite the increase in activity, lending standards remained conservative and did not loosen over the past year. Against this backdrop, the NCREIF/CREFC Open-End Debt Fund Aggregate delivered a 6.1% net*1 total return over the trailing four quarters, but some private real estate debt strategies may have outperformed even more. By comparison, the NCREIF ODCE index generated net total returns of 2.9%*2 and the U.S.-listed REIT index delivered 2.3% gross.*3

Exhibit 1: Annualized return, volatility, and Sharpe ratio, 2014-2025

In our 2026 Inside Real Estate Outlook, we reiterated that commercial real estate (CRE) debt remains our top recommendation across all four quadrants (public vs. private and equity vs. debt). Although increased competition has compressed spreads, we continue to see ample opportunity to originate attractive loans in the year ahead—the Mortgage Bankers Association forecasts total CRE originations to rise +27% in 2026. Risk-adjusted returns remain compelling, supported by conservative loan-to-value ratios on property values that have already reset by roughly 20%.

We also believe the stability of CRE debt funds is underappreciated. As a result, they play an important role in portfolio construction: over the medium to longer term, allocating to CRE debt within a broader mix of private corporate credit or private equity may help to reduce volatility, improve Sharpe ratios, and mitigate drawdown risk. It's possible that these benefits could become even more compelling in the years to come as CRE debt has already experienced a downturn, whereas private corporate credit and private equity have not.

What’s driving the wave of loan originations?

According to the Mortgage Bankers Association (MBA), total CRE mortgage originations increased +27% in 2025 to $634bn. The positive momentum is expected to continue in the year ahead, with originations rising another +27% to $805bn, with core CRE rising +34% and multifamily increasing +21%. Investors often ask why CRE debt markets are gaining momentum even as distress continues to rise. The answer lies in where we are in the cycle and how opportunity is shifting within the market.

  1. We believe the CRE cycle has entered recovery by most traditional measures. Public REIT valuations—often a leading indicator at cyclical turning points—have rebounded meaningfully from their 2023 trough. Private market valuations across major property types have stabilized, and credit markets are once again functioning, supporting renewed transaction activity. Distress, by contrast, is a lagging indicator and continues to rise as the long tail of the downturn works through the system. While elevated delinquencies may weigh on legacy portfolios still absorbing write-downs, they are far less relevant for new funds deploying fresh capital. In this sense, CRE debt has effectively become a “1.0 versus 2.0” market, with new vintages standing to benefit.
  2. The widely discussed wall of maturities represents not just a risk, but a significant opportunity. A large share of loans coming due are refinancing rather than defaulting. Notably, 73% of CMBS conduit loans originally scheduled to mature in 2025 paid off at or before maturity as of the end of 2025, only modestly below the 78% historical average from 2012 to 2024. With approximately $2.1 billion of loans maturing over the next three years, refinancing demand should remain a durable source of origination opportunity.
  3. Transaction activity is recovering. Transaction volumes were 17% higher in the first nine months of 2025 compared with the same period in 2024. Historically, rising volumes have been a reliable signal of improving market confidence, and higher transaction activity directly translates into increased demand for debt capital.
  4. The elevated level of properties delivered in recent years continues to drive financing needs. Newly constructed assets often require bridge loans or permanent take-out financing, adding another layer of demand for CRE debt. While construction activity is expected to moderate going forward, the combination of recent deliveries, loan maturities, and improving transaction volumes supports a robust pipeline of opportunities for lenders.

CRE debt funds have been a clear beneficiary of these dynamics, with their share of total lending rising to a record 14% in 1H25, well above the post-2008 average of roughly 9%. We believe the true share is likely even higher once accounting for the portion of CMBS issuance tied to CRE CLOs, which many CRE debt funds use as financing vehicles. CRE CLO issuance reached its second-highest level since the post-GFC period in 2025, totaling more than $30.5 billion. While competition is increasing, we expect CRE debt funds to remain an integral and durable component of the commercial real estate debt markets.

Exhibit 2: Loan maturities

Attractive risk- and volatility-adjusted returns

Lending standards became less restrictive in 2025 (i.e., less tight), but they did not loosen. This distinction matters. More conservative loan-to-value ratios on property prices that have already reset by roughly 20% help mitigate the risk of further declines in unlevered property valuations. To put the magnitude in perspective, a further reset of this scale would imply cumulative valuation declines of more than 45% this cycle—well beyond the 30%–35% declines experienced in the early 1990s following the Savings & Loans crisis and during the Global Financial Crisis.

Exhibit 3: Sensitivity analysis of hypothetical loan impairment across LTV ranges and CRE price declines

It is also important to remember that CRE mortgages are secured by income-producing real estate—the real estate itself is the collateral, and it does not disappear overnight. In the event of foreclosure, lenders can take ownership of the underlying property at an attractive basis, creating the potential for redevelopment or re-leasing at compelling returns.

We believe this dynamic is a key reason why open-ended CRE debt funds have delivered essentially flat annualized appreciation returns of -0.14% since 2014. The only years with negative returns were -3.1% in 2022, -4.3% in 2023, and -3.9% in 2024, as well as -1.6% in 2020 during COVID. Given our view that the commercial real estate cycle has entered a recovery phase, with prices across major property types having stabilized, we expect appreciation returns for open-ended debt funds to turn positive at some point in 2026.

We believe an even more important consideration is the relative attractiveness of volatility-adjusted returns for CRE debt versus private corporate credit and private equity. The latter two strategies have attracted substantial investor attention over the past decade, largely due to their strong absolute return profiles.

For example, as of 2Q 2025, our analysis of pooled net total returns from Preqin (after management fees and carried interest) shows that North American private equity vehicles (excluding venture capital) have generated 14.6% annualized returns since 1Q 2014, while North American private credit vehicles have delivered 8.7% annualized returns over the same period. By comparison, open-ended CRE debt funds have produced 6.2% annualized total returns, net of fees and promotes, over that same timeframe.

However, CRE debt funds also have much lower volatility than both private corporate credit and private equity over longer periods of time. As a result, we think the portfolio optimization benefits of adding an allocation of CRE debt to either alternative strategy is likely underappreciated. In Exhibit 4, we show modern portfolio statistics (MPTs) at various allocation bands based on a back test of returns since 1Q 2014. In every instance, Sharpe ratios are improved and a drawdown risk is mitigated by adding an allocation of open-ended CRE debt funds to a portfolio of private equity or private credit. While investors do give up some returns, we think it's outweighed by the benefits.

Exhibit 4: Analysis of impacts of various allocations of open-ended CRE debt funds to a portfolio of private equity and private credit (2014 – 3Q25 back test)

Private CRE Credit vs. Private Equity (2014 - 3Q25)

Private CRE Credit vs. Private Credit (2014 - 3Q25)

Source: NCREIF/CREFC, Preqin, Principal Real Estate, 3Q 2025. MDD = max drawdown

Conclusion

Commercial real estate debt delivered strong performance in 2025 and is well-positioned for continued success in 2026 and beyond. With loan originations surging, lending standards remaining conservative, and property valuations having stabilized after meaningful resets, we believe CRE debt offers compelling risk-adjusted returns in today's environment.

The fundamentals supporting CRE debt are on solid footing: a substantial refinancing pipeline, recovering transaction activity, and ongoing financing needs from recent property deliveries should sustain strong origination volumes. Meanwhile, conservative loan-to-value ratios on already-reset property values provide meaningful downside protection—a stark contrast to private corporate credit and private equity markets that have yet to experience a downturn this cycle.

Perhaps most importantly, CRE debt's stability and low volatility make it an effective portfolio diversifier. Our analysis demonstrates that allocating to open-ended CRE debt funds alongside private equity or private credit strategies can improve Sharpe ratios and reduce drawdown risk, even if it means modestly lower absolute returns.

As investors increasingly focus on risk management and portfolio resilience, we believe these benefits position CRE debt as a valuable complement to other private market allocations.

With the commercial real estate cycle having entered recovery and property prices rising again, thereby limiting losses, we maintain our conviction that CRE debt represents one of the most attractive opportunities across both public and private real estate markets today. Indeed, the best vintage returns for CRE debt funds historically occur in the early stages of an early cycle environment.

Exhibit 5: Historical range of IRRs by vintage

Footnotes

*1 Source: NCREIF, 3Q 2025

*2 Source: NCREIF, 4Q 2025

*3 Source: Bloomberg, 4Q 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Brinker International: Revenue Growth, Margin Stability, And Buybacks Reinforce Buy Case
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The scenario that I described in the first article about Brinker International (EAT) is gradually coming true, which leads to an increase in the share price. The target price of $158 has finally been reached, and the stock has increased by 17% since the article was published in late September. I am not changing my view of the company, maintaining the Buy rating, as I see that Chili's business demand is showing an increasingly steady recovery in sales and operating margins from quarter to quarter. The recently released report for Q2 2026 turned out to be successful—the actual financial results were ahead of market expectations. Against this background, I have revised my forecast for revenue growth in 2026, and the updated DCF model indicates a target price of $187 for the year with an upside of 15% to the current price ($162.54 at the time of writing).

Chili’s Drives Outperformance Amid Segment Divergence

The Q2 results not only met market expectations, but also exceeded the forecasts given by analysts. Revenue for the quarter increased by 7% yoy to $1.45 billion, with a consensus forecast of $1.41 billion. At the same time, comparable sales also showed solid growth of 7% yoy. This means that demand remains high, not only for new outlets, but also for old ones. Chili's Grill & Bar made the largest contribution to revenue, with an 8.6% increase in comparable sales. In my last article, I drew attention to the fact that if sales in this segment remain high, this will result in strong financial results for the entire group of companies. In fact, this is what happens—Chili's drags all the growth on itself, while Maggiano's revenue continues to fall from quarter to quarter.

I would like to dig deeper into Chili's revenue growth and understand what this growth consisted of. According to the company, of the 8.6% increase in comparable sales, approximately 4.4% was due to an increase in menu prices, another 2.7% was due to an increase in the number of guests, and the remaining 1.5% was due to the fact that visitors on average spent more money per visit by ordering additional items on their menu. These results suggest that the growth of the business is balanced by a simultaneous increase in the price of the menu and an increase in the number of guests. If the growth was purely due to prices, and the number of visitors was falling and vice versa, then there would be less positive in the results obtained. I hope for sustained growth in demand. Yes, in general, compared to the growth rate of total revenue in Q1, the pace slowed down slightly in Q2, but this is rather normalization, after a sharp jump. I don't see anything negative in this.

Across the entire business, operating profit increased by 7.7% yoy to $168 million. However, it was not possible to achieve an increase in operating margin this quarter. This is due to a drop in margins at Maggiano's from 22.7% to 16.0%. At least Chili's margin growth from 18.7% to 19.1% helped keep it at the same level. Throughout the report, it is clear that the entire business is now, in fact, based on Chili's restaurants. The cost of food has increased slightly, but the share of salaries and insurance costs in revenue has decreased. Plus, the company increased marketing expenses, which also affected operating margins.

At the net profit level, the results are also positive. Net income increased from $118.5 million to $128.5 million, and GAAP earnings per share increased from $2.61 to $2.86. Adjusted EPS increased slightly from $2.80 to $2.87, but was 12% higher than the consensus forecast. Both revenue and operating profit growth and a reduction in interest expenses from $14.7 million to $10.7 million helped. Overall, the quarterly results were strong, but the problem with Maggiano's remains, and it is this part of the business that is dragging the final financial results down. If the management manages to restore Maggiano's and maintain steady demand at Chili's, then this will be the next strong factor for the stock to be revalued upwards. Let's look at the forecasts.

Revised Targets Signal Sustainable Operating Momentum

Management has raised its forecasts for the whole of 2026. Now the company expects revenue of $5.76–$5.83 billion against the previous range of $5.60–$5.70 billion and adjusted EPS of $10.45–$10.85 against the previous range of $9.90–$10.50. All thanks to the increased sales of Chili's restaurants and the increase in prices for menu items. At the same time, the management does not expect a strong price increase - rather a low single-digit growth. But the price of beef may rise in the second watering of the year, which can reduce margins if it is not possible to transfer this increase to the menu. Management also expects to allocate less funds to capex than originally planned, in the range of $250-260 million. This is a positive thing for investors, as free cash flow will be higher. Wall Street expects revenue growth of 7.94% to $5.81 billion and EPS of $10.70 (+20.20%) for the year. It turns out that analysts expect higher revenue growth in 2026 than management, and the consensus on EPS is within the forecast range of management. Revenue growth of 3.50% to $1.48 billion and adjusted EPS of $2.88 (+8.40%) is forecast in Q1.

I generally agree with the management's forecast and consider it to be quite realistic and achievable. It will be possible to get ahead of these forecasts if revenue and margin at Maggiano's are restored. Financial results in Q3 will continue to normalize after the surge in Q1: I expect revenue in the range of $1.47-1.51 billion, and adjusted EPS in the range of $2.86-2.88.

DCF-Implied Upside with Conservative Growth Assumptions

In this chapter, I will talk about my DCF model, its prerequisites, and final valuation. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2026-2030, discounting the forecast FCFS, then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030) and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and terminal value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap). With EV/EBITDA, this is the enterprise value from which net debt is also subtracted), which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

I have revised the revenue growth rate towards a slight improvement. The revenue CAGR for 2026-2030 will be 4%. My forecast is based on comparable sales growth of +2-3% per year and 1-2% prices in the medium term, along with the targeted opening of new restaurants. According to my model, the operating margin will grow to 13.5-14% by 2030 due to cost control. But the margin will not be able to grow stronger due to cost inflation and high competition in the market. CapEx will stay in the range of 4-5% of total revenue, which will support business growth and upgrade old restaurants. As a result, the average FCF yield for the period in 2026-2030 will be 10.50% versus 6.47% for 2021-2025.

I discounted the forecast FCF for the WACC at 8.28% (all prerequisites are shown in the table above).

As prices rise, so do the multiples. Despite this, discounts to the sector average remain in many multiples. The same applies to discounts to the five-year averages of the company itself. This suggests that there is no revaluation in stocks and the stock price still has room to grow. The current price is still attractive to buy based on a comparative analysis of the multiples.

Both valuation methods show almost identical results. In the baseline scenario, the fair share price is $187-195 with a 15-20% growth potential. The most positive scenario is an upside of 27-30%, and a negative one is an upside of 4-12%. As a result, I don't see any overestimation in Brinker shares now, either after comparative evaluation by multiples or by calculations of the DCF model. In my opinion, they are still undervalued and have potential for growth. I keep the buy rating and recommend buying stocks.

Leverage Within Comfort Range, EPS Tailwind from Repurchases

The debt burden on the business is quite manageable. At the end of Q2, the company's total debt was $1.76 billion, with cash in the accounts in the region of $15 million. This results in a net debt of $1.75 million. The debt/EBITDA ratio is only 2.18x, while the net debt/EBITDA is 2.16x. Operating profit exceeds interest expenses by almost 13 times. Traditionally, the company does not pay dividends, but shares profits with shareholders through share buybacks. Approximately $100 million worth of shares were repurchased in Q2, and $235 million since the beginning of fiscal 2026. At the same time, the board of directors expanded the program by another $400 million, and the company still has about $315 million available limit. In Q3, the company will continue to buy back shares from the market, which has a positive impact on EPS.

Risks

The main risk, in my opinion, may be related to inflated market expectations. If investors get used to the double-digit growth rates that were in Q1, then the normalization of results may be disappointing. On the other hand, based on analysts' forecasts, it is clear that the market does not have any inflated expectations for revenue and net profit at the moment. Falling sales at Chili's will hit revenue hard, as it is the only part of the business that is growing steadily and dragging the overall results with it. Maggiano's ongoing downturn also poses additional risks to the business, negatively impacting revenue and overall margins. The remaining risks are related to high competition, macroeconomics, regulation, and so on.

Final Thoughts

In my opinion, Brinker shares still have room to grow, especially if financial results continue to outpace market expectations. At the same time, the company has enough problems, but if the management manages to turn the situation around in some parts of the business, then we can see a new round of acceleration of revenue and net profit. I keep the Buy rating.

data.title: Snowflake: Transition Story Priced As Growth, But Momentum Is Missing
data.description:
Snowflake (SNOW) today is in a transition - it is not a hypergrowth SaaS story, I believe that the market is trying to understand what it should be. Also, it does not help that the market is currently on a witch hunt and devaluing SaaS companies. In my opinion, Snowflake is being valued as a slowing momentum company, mainly due to revenue growth slowing from 30% to 27-29%, insider selling. While fundamentals should show strength—125% net revenue retention, 37% RPO growth (which is above revenue momentum), 25% free cash flow margin—I question whether these metrics are going to be enough to justify the valuation.

Overall, my thesis is simple. Snowflake's revenue model is based on consumption, I do not believe that AI-driven consumption will scale fast enough to offset that structural deceleration in core warehousing growth. As I am trading momentum, or special situations, I have to say that this is not a momentum trade. It is a transition story, but transition stories tend to underperform before proving themselves. In the current situation and market conditions, risk-to-reward does not look attractive even for conservative positioning.

Growth Is Misunderstood

Snowflake revenue growth slowing from 30% to 27-29% for many investors looks like a structural slowdown. But I think that conclusion like that is just too simplified. At more than $4.4 billion in annual revenue base, almost 30% growth means expansion, which is bigger than last year, when the stock was hyper-growing. I do not think this is collapse; I think this is just a natural scale effect. Still, reality is that deceleration in consumption-based models can be more persistent than one would expect

It is important that this slowdown happened after the client cost optimization cycle in 2023-2024, when the companies were actively decreasing cloud costs and reviewed them there. However, I am afraid that optimization behavior could become a structural discipline rather than a temporary thing. Although I really like RPO growth of 37%, backlog does not automatically translate into revenue jumps. If AI remains experimental or limited, the expected acceleration may be slower and, to be fair, weaker than bulls expect.

I know that one of the strongest bullish arguments is remaining performance obligations (RPO), which was growing, as I mentioned, 37%. At first sight it is very positive news; when the order base is growing faster than revenue, then we can make a conclusion that future revenue is going to be stronger and consumption should follow. However, Snowflake model is built not only on fixed subscriptions, but also on consumption. This means, that RPO is not guaranteeing future cash flow; it all depends on platform usage. The companies can have the credits purchased or just committed to the service, but in reality, they can use those credits very slowly, especially if IT is trying to save the budget. RPO means that the company has a backlog of contracts with big clients, but it does not mean that the clients are now using the services more. If AI solutions stay more in the testing stage and are not used for daily work, I believe that the gap between revenue and RPO could shrink. In other words, revenue growth would be slowing.

FCF And Capital Efficiency

I have seen people refer to Snowflake as a high quality business due to its 25% free cash flow margin. Nearing a billion dollars in annual FCF level, the company does not seem to be an early stage growth story. From a capital efficiency perspective, I have to agree; this argument is very strong - margin like this shows that pricing power is good and the scale effect is in place.

I like to pick stocks using GARP logic. That means I am searching for growth with real profitability. Here, real profitability (net income) has not been reached; also, you can notice that the company is paying employees in shares. Even though FCF is positive, part of this effect comes from accounting, and not a fully mature, stable business model. If the company continues to issue new shares, the real value for shareholders could be lower than FCF shows.

Also, high FCF margin today does not mean it's going to be the same tomorrow. Generative AI is capital heavy, and if the cost structure becomes less favorable, pressure on margins can appear faster than we expect. I understand that capital effectiveness is a good argument and catalyst itself, but it is still not good enough to ignore structural risks.

Valuation

Snowflake's valuation is interesting. Historically the company was traded at around 40-50x EV/Sales, but after a few years, it decreased to 11-12x. Given this, I see only one conclusion: that this is not euphoric AI valuation anymore, more of a disciplined growth regime. I have done a quite conservative DCF using 10-11% WACC and 2-3% terminal growth, which shows that the company is overvalued.

The problem is that assumptions like these might be too early. Snowflake today is generating a 25% free cash flow margin and is nearing a billion dollar FCF level. If in 3-5 years revenue CAGR stays around 25% and margins expand, the company like this should be again entering the growth stage, but it does not. Also, it's worth mentioning that realistically it is better to use terminal value calculations through FCF multiple.

Stocks in the AI topic tend to show S-curve logic. AI infrastructure cycles rarely experience linear development. The first phase is always experimenting and optimization, which the market often interprets as growth slowdown. The second phase is scale, when the consumption model starts to generate disproportionate revenue jumps. If Snowflake AI (Cortex and partnership integrations) transitions to this second phase, the current valuation of mine, would only show cycles bottoming and not overvaluation.

P.S. I cannot stress this enough, but given that SaaS stocks are getting beaten by the market, the current valuation might cause sharp corrections, so plan your trades accordingly. Plus, stock this year is in a downtrend, I would not be catching a falling knife until the price makes a base, or earnings prove growth that institutions would use to start accumulating.

Catalysts

Let's talk now about catalysts. The narrative today is AI consumption as a new structural growth catalyst. The logic is simple: if the company starts using generative AI, agents, and natural language requests in their databases, this means that there will be more compute requests, more credit consumption, and more revenue for Snowflake. The consumption model in this case works as leverage, as every additional AI query is directly monetized.

However, the question is not whether AI will exist. The question is whether it will become a massive, constantly used enterprise layer. The majority of companies are already testing their AI solutions, often in limited teams, I remember the bank I used to work at 2 years ago already had quite advanced AI, but it was still a pilot project. This does not necessarily mean that consumption will become an immediate catalyst, yet, I believe it will eventually.

I mentioned briefly that AI is capital intensive, and if Snowflake has to share the margin with infrastructure suppliers or offer better, more aggressive pricing, just to get AI consumption, then revenue growth could be suppressed. In other words, AI could actually increase volumes, but not necessarily improve unit cost economics.

On February 25, 2026, after market close, Snowflake will be announcing earnings. I have baked this into the DCF model, but analysts expect 27-28% product revenue growth. Consensus for EPS has decreased, and I saw some expecting negative or very weak results, even though earlier they had beat the expectations.

The Market

As of February, the market is really hard to understand. On the one hand, the overall market still clings to AI optimism, as we can see from the S&P climbing to 7000, which was clearly driven by the AI narrative and expectations of monetary easing. On the other hand, in the short term, the market is acting up; there are many talks that the AI cycle is becoming an expensive CAPEX game. In recent days we saw many stocks correct and investors entering a risk-off regime with AI stocks.

The macro catalyst for Snowflake would be the Fed lowering interest rates. Still, market expectations are sensitive to any macro number these days. For a stock like SNOW decreasing rates are very good, because the software/AI application layer could get another catch-up wave; also, it could get amplified if SNOW shows real consumption acceleration results.

Summary

For now, until I see results, I am rating the company as a sell, or at least avoid new positions. The risk is simple: if Snowflake does not provide good and clear guidance or revenue stabilization, or AI consumption acceleration evidence, the market could interpret that as a slowing down of SaaS. Even a small miss in this market causes disproportionate sell-offs, especially if the sentiment is negative and valuations are stretched. Given the current downtrend and sensitive macroeconomic environment, short-term risks are way higher than rewards.

data.title: Strategic Pivot Opens Opportunity For Investors In GFL Environmental
data.description:
Some people say, 'One man's trash is another man's treasure,' and in my opinion, this is a classic example of those words in action. GFL Environmental (GFL) has shifted from a high-growth startup in the municipal solid waste (MSW) industry to what seems like a solid long term compounder with several catalysts on the horizon. Long-term investors can jump in now and enjoy a potentially very lucrative ride on the endless amounts of waste we generate.

Who is GFL Environmental?

GFL is the fourth-largest environmental services company in North America. It was founded in 2007 by its current CEO Patrick Dovigi, who still owns roughly 7% of the company. His multiple voting shares (of which he is the 100% owner) still allow him to flex up to 26% of the voting power, which ensures his vision for the company translates into action, despite his much lower direct equity stake.

The company operates in the USA (25 states) and Canada and generates over CAD$6.5bn in revenue. They make their money in solid waste management, and they generate this revenue through the collection, transport, transfer, recycling, and disposal of non-hazardous waste.

I love businesses like this. We need them, period. Dot-com bubble? Still need my waste removed, GFC? Still. Covid? Yep. In good times and bad, your waste needs to be 'taken care of.' The best part, their clients are generally large and sign long-term contracts with built-in escalators often linked to things like inflation or fuel costs.

Why buy now?

At its 2025 investor day, the company had some really big news. In what I'd like to call a 'sign of maturity,' they announced a strategic pivot. This leopard was changing its spots, and instead of maintaining its history as an aggressive, high-growth consolidator in its space, it was going to simplify. The focus was to change to a streamlined, highly efficient, pure play solid waste company. Its formative years of aggressive growth had helped them reach scale. Now it's time to focus on quality compounding off a strong balance sheet. The plan has had almost a year to progress and evolve, and I've decided to post their results earlier this week. I'm going to build out my position fully. Here's why.

The company has a bold plan that encompasses both obtaining an investment-grade credit rating AND being included in large Canadian and US indices (the S&P 500 is the ultimate goal). Its journey to achieve this requires, amongst other things, a shift, in which focus changes from 'growth at all costs' to quality growth, and the journey started early last year with the decision to divest from its more volatile liquid waste and soil remediation business called 'Environmental Services.' They retained a 44% equity stake in the company, but the sale of the majority of shares (to Apollo and BC Partners) in a deal valued at CAD 8bn was the first step in the process. The second step was to aggressively deleverage the balance sheet. In order to obtain an investment-grade credit rating, the company has to prove that its lower leverage is a permanent feature of the business and not just a temporary one off event. This means they need to demonstrate Net Debt/EBITDA below 4x for a prolonged period. They also need to show consistent free cash flow after CAPEX and interest.

The sale of 56% of Environmental Services yielded them C$6.2bn. C$3.75bn of which was used to expunge debt. In a one-two punch, this simultaneously reduced leverage from 5.3x to 4.3x and reduced interest expenses by a whopping C$200m. This went a very long way in boosting operating and FCF generation.

They also committed to using C$2.25bn to buy back shares (subject to market conditions), which has subsequently been done, and it was upped to a total of C$3bn or 10% of the company. (The share price had a very lackluster year last year, making this quite opportune). All shares repurchased have subsequently been cancelled.

Results out on Wednesday made pleasant reading. Net leverage at year end had dropped further to 3.4x, the lowest in the company's history. FCF jumped by 23.6% yoy to C$755.9m, the highest in the company's history. Looking ahead to guidance for the new year, we can expect FCF growth of 14% to C$860m on a constant currency basis and leverage to end the year in the 'low 3s.' This puts them on track for the first potential catalyst, which is the investment grade rating.

The next goal is to obtain a place in the S&P 500. This is a bit trickier; the requirements for S&P 500 inclusion vary from quantitative things such as US Domicile, US listing, liquidity, financial scale, and profitability. With their announcement a few weeks back that they are shifting their domicile to Miami Beach, Florida, a pretty big hurdle has just been overcome. With this shift, they will also change their reporting methodology to US GAAP from IFRS. This aligns them to US reporting standards, which enables direct comparisons to US peers such as Waste Management (WM), Clean Harbors (CLH), Republic Services (RSG), Waste Connections (WCN), and Casella Waste (CWST). Once this happens, they will be more easily distinguishable for US investors.

The toughest ask for the inclusion is their size. Once all the quantitative and subjective boxes are ticked (assuming they are), the single largest impediment is their market cap, which at the time of writing is just under $15bn which is about $7bn or 50%, higher than their current share price.

Valuation

So can they get there? Let's look at the numbers. Companies in the Waste Management space are valued on EV/EBITDA, and the sector trades at an average EV/EBITDA of 14.8x. On the results out Wednesday and at the closing price of $41.33 the company trades at 13.6x, with an FWD multiple that drops to 11.96x.

This places them at a large discount to not only the sector average but also their own average long-term multiple of 16.2x. The above calculations are based on consensus forecasts for both revenue and margins, and if these prove to be correct, there is a value discount to be had here.

The catalysts here are almost self-reinforcing. As the company transforms into a high-quality compounder in the industry, its valuation should trend higher; this in turn is reinforced by the steps being taken to obtain an investment-grade rating. The other way to think about it is, that if the company obtains its investment-grade rating, it should deserve a higher multiple. How much higher? Well, the above numbers don't seem to be a stretch to me because they're based on actual group and historic valuations.

This implies that the stock is trading at an immediate discount of 14% to the group and 26% to its long-term average.

Fast forward three years out, and the target prices range between $70 and $87, which implies returns of 70% to 87% from here.

These gains in share prices, should they occur, then culminate in the company reaching the size required to enter the S&P500, which then potentially results in a further rerating as tracker funds purchase and the company gets elevated into more prestigious company.

Risks

There are, of course, the normal market risks and business risks associated with these companies. More specific to them, however, is that they don't follow through on the plan. In a previous life this company operated as a highly leveraged consolidator, which made them a riskier play. A stretched balance sheet made them more exposed to higher interest rates, or an economic slowdown, for example. Provided they execute as promised, I see no reason to doubt the upside potential here.

Conclusion

GFL is fast becoming a high-quality business with lower leverage and strong cash flows. The company is on track to achieve its investment-grade rating, which should result in premium expansion. Further down the line, the prospect of large index inclusions adds to potential alpha. I rate GFL a strong buy for patient, long-term investors that like catalyst-rich opportunities.

data.title: Private Equity's Volume Of Software Deals Slowed As AI Risks Grew
data.description:
The pace of private equity and venture capital investment in application software slowed for at least three consecutive years amid rising concerns about the risks advances in artificial intelligence could pose to software company growth.

Application software deals backed by private equity and venture capital firms totaled 3,665 globally in 2025, down 21% from 4,638 deals in 2024, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Deal volume declined for at least the third consecutive year in 2025, while aggregate deal value was the highest total since 2022 at $148.72 billion.

The median value of private equity transactions in the sector rose to $280 million in 2025, higher than the $241.8 million median recorded in 2022, the year ChatGPT was released, the data shows.

Alternative asset managers' exposure to the software sector was under the spotlight after an early-February sell-off of technology stocks. The market turbulence was the result of rising anxiety among public market investors that AI-powered automation could trim software company growth and erode valuations in the sector, said Scott Denne, a senior research analyst at 451 Research.

"What is happening is that they don't feel like they have clarity on how to price the risk of AI," Denne said.

Estimating exposure

Private markets managers have been grappling with those same uncertainties for years, according to Ares Management Corp. CEO Michael Arougheti.

"It is quite odd to us that the public markets have woken up to AI disruption as a theme. If you've been investing over the last five-plus years and you haven't been thinking about opportunities and risks created from technology and AI implementation, you've probably been asleep at the switch," Arougheti said in a Feb. 10 conference interview.

A week earlier, on Ares' fourth-quarter 2025 earnings call, Arougheti said investments in software companies accounted for about 6% of Ares' approximately $622.5 billion in assets under management.

Software exposure reported by private equity's Big Four listed firms in February ranged from 2% to 7% of AUM. Apollo Global Management Inc. (APO) estimated its exposure at the low end of that range, while Blackstone Inc. (BX) and KKR & Co. Inc. (KR) were at the high end. The Carlyle Group Inc. (CG) put its exposure to software at 6% of AUM.

Vintage risk

Private equity and venture capital exits from application software investments have accelerated in recent years, increasing to 605 exits in 2025 from 516 in 2024 and 421 exits in 2023, according to Market Intelligence data. The pattern mirrors a broader uptick in private equity exits since 2023.

The age of software investments that remain in private equity portfolios is becoming a significant factor in their vulnerability to AI disruption, said Nehal Raj, co-managing partner of TPG Capital LP, TPG Inc.'s private equity business.

"Where you will see more risk is in companies that were underwritten 2018, 2019, 2020 — prior to the advent of generative AI. And those [fund] vintages are more susceptible to risk and disruption," Raj said on the firm's fourth-quarter 2025 earnings call.

On the same call, TPG CEO Jon Winkelried estimated the firm's overall software exposure at 11% of AUM. Within TPG's private equity business, exposure is 18% of AUM, he said.

Upside, downside

Blackstone CFO Michael Chae said AI advances will result in a "range of outcomes over time" for software companies and their investors, both good and bad.

"We expect larger, well-entrenched businesses to be better protected and in many cases, beneficiaries of AI given the attractive moats and resources to invest in their own businesses and apply AI as a competitive advantage," Chae said.

Business moats that separate software companies from competitors are a key factor in which businesses are best positioned to sustain value — and perhaps even thrive — as AI roils the industry, said Dhaval Moogimane, hi-tech and software industry lead for consulting firm West Monroe.

Examples include software companies operating in highly regulated environments that are difficult for upstarts to penetrate, Moogimane said. Software businesses with proprietary data or proprietary knowledge of customer workflows and business processes are also less at risk of AI disruption, but they have to stay on guard, he added.

"The reality is you can't sit still on that moat. You'll have to act on that moat, and extend your moat over time," Moogimane said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Walmart: Defensive Compounder With Omnichannel Margin Upside
data.description:
Thesis

After decades of loyal customers, I think it is fair to ask if Walmart Inc. (WMT) has reached its peak. CEO Doug McMillon ends his reign and will be replaced by John Furner in February in a planned leadership transition. Since 2009, the stock has quadrupled, beating the S&P 500 with an all-time high revenue of $681 billion last year. WMT has defended its market leadership position against intensifying competition. WMT’s scale, price leadership, and omnichannel execution still make it challenging for competitors to take market share. Since WMT is a mature company with recent revenue growth of ~3.2% and an EBIT margin of ~4.1%, it should be considered as a high-durability compounder, and this durability factor could motivate a premium in uncertain macro environments.

John Furner, Doug McMillion’s successor, is a long-tenured internal executive who has already led the core business of WMT, which reduces execution risk and strengthens strategic consistency. WMT’s leadership transition further supports continuity rather than disruption in its company journey.

I rate WMT as a long-term 'Buy' because investors are not simply paying for a growth case here, but rather a resilient, mature firm that has consistent free cash flows and capital returns and also a possible path to margin improvements via mix shifting. It should therefore be underwritten as a defensive consumer compounder stock that suits a long-term investor.

I would be comfortable leaving the stock in the drawer and holding it for the long term. I would only revise my valuation estimates if WMT were to break its long-term trend by showing a noticeable decline in revenue and earnings.

Market Outlook

The underlying market is estimated to grow at a CAGR of 3.3% during 2026-2035, with a valuation of $2.49 trillion by 2035. Since there is a single-digit growth in the underlying market, this supports WMT’s ability to sustain low single-digit growth through different economic cycles. The U.S. retail environment is in a predictable but slow phase of growth. The overall market is expected to remain in a low single-digit range, and consumption is believed to continue to benefit from stable demand and demographic overall growth in the economy.

Malls are also losing market share to open-air retail parks, which are dominated by the larger chains such as Target Corporation (TGT), Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) and WMT. Traditional malls continue to lose traffic, and consumers are migrating towards open-air retail formats/e-commerce such as WMT. This reinforces WMT’s physical footprint as a strategic asset.

Grocery is a defensive spending category with a stable upward long-run trend in U.S. grocery sales, rising steadily except for a spike in pandemic-driven stocking. After this, growth rates have stabilized and returned to normal.

A stable catalyst for WMT’s continued growth is a steady population increase that directly supports long-term baseline demand for consumer goods. Grocery and household goods benefit from population-driven volume growth regardless of economic cycles. Since the total U.S. population has increased from roughly 250 million in the early 1990s to over 340 million today, the overall addressable demand for everyday necessities, food, and other consumables structurally increases. This demographic factor speaks for a larger growing base that supports further slow, stable growth. In combination with WMT’s leading market share and large footprint in the markets, it further reinforces the company’s ability to compound revenue steadily without risky international exposure. As a large player, WMT can, through small volume growth, translate this to good operating leverage with automation and process efficiency. WMT already has a built-in distribution and store network and can still gain better than opponents on this long-term trend.

Moreover, e-commerce retail sales are steadily growing, and it is noted how consumers have shifted in their shopping behavior towards online and omnichannel purchasing. The estimated CAGR for e-commerce is currently 8.9%. For WMT, this is an opportunity through its omnichannel economics to reap more market share in the e-commerce field. The firm’s advantage also lies in integrating digital demand with its store footprint, where the customer can pick up orders or use same-day delivery. WMT is well-positioned to capture this consumer behavior trend without aggressive physical expansion in new stores.

Chatbots

According to the Financial Times, bots are now learning how to shop, which will increase AI sales and now accounts for 1.5% of U.S. retail e-commerce in 2026. Walmart is already operationalizing AI and has deployed Sparky, which helps the customer in interacting with the store and can lower staff costs.

Google Gemini and Walmart launched a collaboration in January that further strengthens Walmart's AI journey. Here, the transition will be from traditional web or app searches to agent-led shopping, which represents the evolution of shopping. Last quarter, sales in e-commerce grew by 28%, which marks the seventh consecutive quarter with growth above 20%.

Walmart leadership has described scenarios where AI recognizes meal preparation, household projects, or seasonal needs and reduces the number of steps required to complete a purchase. This further speaks for a changing behavior with a new modern consumer buying wares in new ways, which WMT is adjusting very well to and can profit from.

Financial analysis

Relative valuation

Since WMT is operating in several product areas, I choose to make several buckets, which can help to examine WMT’s valuation position even further to help my readers as much as possible. I find an “all retail” bucket to not be 100% accurate since WMT also offers, for example, home improvement, electronics, auto parts, and more. All those product areas have a bit different margins, cyclicality, and capital intensity.

By looking at the “closest” retail competitors, which reflect WMT’s core business the best, we can see that WMT takes the lead on all three multiples: EV/Sales, EV/EBITDA, and P/E. It is only beaten by Loblaw Companies on its EV/sales slightly and Kroger on its P/E number. This implies a premium to its core peers, and I find it to be justified with their strong product mix, brand, and scale. However, it leaves less room for alarming news with lower margins or stagnated growth.

Compared to its core grocery peer group average, Walmart trades at an estimated 64% premium on P/E and 42% premium on EV/Sales, highlighting the market’s willingness to assign a quality premium for Walmart’s earnings and sales durability.

In the second bucket, WMT trades slightly higher across all peers when looking at membership clubs, which I use as a quality reference, whereas customers are prepared to pay a fee for being able to buy at those clubs.

Meanwhile, in the discount store bucket, WMT trades slightly lower on EV/sales but at a premium on both EV/EBITDA and P/E. It is not a clear anchor for WMT’s value but gives some guidance as a secondary reference to understand the valuation universe a bit better in this case.

I find it interesting that Amazon is valued less on its P/E of 33.65x compared to 41.81x for WMT. Amazon’s lower P/E reflects a materially different business mix and margin structure with different growth intervals. The e-commerce bucket is mainly here to frame competitive intensity and strategic optionality in e-commerce. E-commerce models and platforms have fundamentally different segment mixes and other margin drivers than WMT’s store-led business.

Concurrently, in the specialty retail bucket, it is noted that both EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA come in a bit higher than WMT. Investors are paying less for future profit with a lower P/E ratio. The last bucket, with specialty retail stores, clearly has different main core business areas but still has some interesting characteristics, which is why I choose to have it as a reference in a broader retail context. It is noted that investors are willing to pay a premium for WMT.

Discounted Cash Flow

Since WMT has left its double-digit growth behind and become a single-digit steady growth engine, I’d use the geometric average revenue/EBIT growth of the last five years in my base case and hone those numbers for conservative and optimistic case estimations. I base assumptions on Walmart’s history and management guidance, and I normalize volatile items like CAPEX toward a steady-state level to avoid overstating long-run reinvestment.

To visualize how WMT can be affected by different inputs, I choose to build a three-scenario model that shows a conservative, base, and optimistic scenario. I value Walmart using an FCFF (free cash flow to the firm) DCF because it allows valuation of the entire enterprise and is consistent with discounting at WACC.

Calculation of WACC

TGR

Current inflation in the U.S. is at 2.7% as of December 2025, and GDP growth is at 4.4%. If I look at the workings by Aswath Damodaran in “Historical (Compounded Annual) Growth Rates by Sector” I get a 3.22% growth rate for Retail (Grocery and Food) which I use as an input for my base case scenario. I think it is pretty reasonable that the sector grows a bit higher than the inflation but lower than the economy as a whole.

Sensitivity Analysis

The DCF model is very sensitive to small changes in the WACC and terminal growth rate, which affect the value heftily.

Base scenario

Under the conservative case, the implied value is $128.5 per share, or roughly 9% upside from today’s price. The base case implies $156.4 per share, or 33% upside. Under the optimistic case, fair value is $190.7 per share, or a 62% upside, which implies a spread between 9% and +62%. I rate BUY for long-term investors with a multi-year horizon because of small margin upsides, durability, and capital returns that are being compounded over time.

Financial strength

By the end of 2029, it is estimated that Amazon and WMT together could control approximately 66% of the retail sales online, which suggests continued growth and reinforces WMT’s long-term growth positioning and scale advantage.

Moreover, WMT also now for the first time crossed the ~$1 trillion market cap, making its way into the Nasdaq 100 index as of January 20th. This further strengthens mechanical buying from index funds and ETFs that track the benchmark and can promote liquidity in the stock.

Risks

Margins: WMT operates in a low-margin environment with intensive price competition. Particularly in groceries and consumables, aggressive pricing strategies are being used to defend market share, which could pressure operating margins if costs are not fully passed on to customers. With WMT’s large revenue base, even small contractions can impact free cash flow generation and the operating income.

Cost Inflation and Labor Risk: The company’s large workforce exposes WMT to regulatory labor changes, wage inflation, and benefit cost pressures. Even though automation and more efficient production offset parts of these effects, labor costs could challenge margin expansion later on.

E-commerce margin risk: WMT is heavily reliant on operational efficiencies to scale as expected. Profitability in e-commerce is a key variable for a long-term valuation upside. Increased infrastructure investments or last-mile delivery expenses could limit margin expansion if operational efficiency lowers.

Competitive risk: It is a highly competitive landscape with both traditional retailers and also digital platforms that are aggressively competing on delivery speed, price, and convenience. Elevated capital expenditures from a sustained competitive landscape might further pressure returns.

Relative valuation multiple risk: According to the relative valuation section, particularly the first bucket that stands for traditional grocery peers, WMT currently trades at a valuation premium, which reflects its omnichannel growth strategy and defensive earnings profile.

Upcoming earnings release guidance

Moving over to the upcoming earnings release on February 19, FactSet consensus estimates FY2026 sales at ~712.6 billion with a YoY growth of ~4-5% and EBIT margin of ~4.3%. I'm expecting WMT to be able to use AI well in its business and might come in a bit higher than consensus due to its AI strategy. Investor focus is mainly going to be on conserving margins and revenue growth for this mature quality stock. Margin developments, cost inflation, and pricing competition will be monitored closely by investors, as will progress on e-commerce and management comments on the latest growth expectations.

Conclusion

Although WMT is currently trading with a valuation premium compared to retail peers and most other buckets in my set, this premium is justified by strong, stable, steady cash flows and long-term compounding qualities. WMT’s durability compounding and incremental margin upside support make this an interesting case. I believe WMT to be in a position where it can improve its leverage over time and monetize its digital ecosystem.

All taken together, I leave a BUY recommendation for long-term investors seeking exposure to a high-quality consumer staple stock with defensive characteristics.

data.title: Intrum AB: Servicing Business Is Strengthening - But Long-Term Challenges Persist
data.description:
Introduction

Intrum (ITJTY) (INJJF) is down 80% the past 5 years, mainly due to its high leverage and the rising interest expenses that put pressure on Intrum's solvency. There have been some improvements and more clarity around their debt issues lately, and Intrum has also seen growth accelerating in Servicing segment, which is a positive. However, there are still concerns regarding the debt issue, and that is still priced into the company. In this article I will go through why I believe Intrum is a HOLD, despite positive improvements lately.

What has happened?

Over the past years, Intrum has had flat growth. To understand that, we have to understand Intrum's business model. Intrum's credit management services, also called CMS or Servicing, collect revenue by servicing and collecting credit on behalf of banks, telecoms, corporations, and other businesses. Servicing is their asset-light business, and Intrum earns revenue from recurring fees from the services they provide. The past couple of years, more companies have had debt and credit challenges due to increasing interest rates and inflation, and this, in theory, would have driven more growth, but that has not been the case. This is due to two main factors: Servicing is a stable business and there is lower investment into NPL portfolios.

Servicing has long-term contracts, and fees are stable and recurring. Servicing revenue streams have been stable and have not had a big impact from increasing interest rates. Also, PI investments are down because Intrum has been focusing more on balance sheets and deleveraging. Lower focus on investments has improved cash flows, but it is impacting growth with lower gross collections. Intrum sold parts of its back book back in 2024, and this has driven growth down but improved the balance sheet. This choice was ultimately the right decision, as Intrum struggled with high leverage and interest payments. They changed from short-term growth but with higher risk to short-term stabilisation but delayed growth.

Now over Q4 and FY 2025. Intrum reported a decrease of 7% YoY in income, mainly due to FX changes and a smaller investment book. Management stated that this was a currency effect and not a demand problem, as they saw organic growth in Servicing and better-than-expected performance in Investing, we will look more closely at this later. C/I ratio stands at 134% in Q4; that is up 47% YoY. This is not a good improvement, but currency effects and reduced investment book impact C/I, because they have managed to improve Adj. EBIT margin significantly since 2024, and this is due to cost reductions, but not enough to offset the top-line decline in 2025. FY 2025 C/I is at 101% compared to 92% in 2024.

If we look more closely at servicing the past quarters, the trend has changed over the past two quarters, with positive organic growth YoY.

However, while growth has been flat until more recently, they have improved margins consistently upwards. Their Adj. EBIT margin has now reached 25% RTM; the trend is quite clear over the past quarters. Intrum overall has managed to reduce costs by 2.5 billion SEK since Q2 2024. The reduction is driven by cost discipline and mainly personnel reduction. This shows their business model is getting more efficient, and they are able to reduce personnel. This is a part of their strategy going forward: reach higher efficiency by utilizing new tools and software.

Adj. Servicing EBIT grew 3% YoY, mainly impacted by a 2 ppt higher EBIT margin of 31%. Income fell YoY, mainly impacted by FX, as organic growth grew 1%, compared to last year, where it fell 6% YoY.

Investing adj. EBIT fell 14% YoY, and this is due to the divestments. Intrum has been more selective with their investments, and this has been the right choice. They have prioritised quality above volume. In 2025 they invested 1.2 billion SEK with a blended IRR of 20% compared to 19% last year.

Intrum managed a stronger return compared to the forecast. 3% return above the active forecast and 9% above the original forecast. This shows performance on their investing book is still strong.

Intrum has had exceptional performance in different regions the past year. All regions saw strong EBIT margin expansion, with Northern Europe at 10.4%, and the worst was Eastern Europe, which only had a margin expansion of 3%. Southern Europe is their biggest servicing market, almost twice as big as both Middle Europe and Northern Europe, and Southern Europe has the strongest margins of 31.7% in 2025.

Growth

Intrum introduced their 2030 strategy in the recent quarter. They set several long-term targets to improve servicing EBIT, continue to reduce cost levels, and achieve a leverage ratio defined by EBITDA/Net Debt of 3x by 2030.

They are set to achieve these goals by higher automation, stronger integration of AI and scaling. Intrum operations are approximately 10% automated, which is similar to the industry average. This is an example of how under-automated the sector is, and there can be huge efficiency gains made here over time. Additionally, Intrum's size can help them bring new technology quickly through different regions, and being a big player in Europe is an advantage, as Intrum can spend more on new, expensive technology that smaller players cannot justify. Intrum wants to be the "go-to" partner for credit risk, not only for debt collections but also to expand into new industries and bring more value-added services to Intrum's customers. Intrum wants to be the number one investing partner, through strong deal flow and best-in-class underwriting skills. Because of Intrum's scale, they have lots of knowledge across more than 20 markets. This gives them access to strong data points, which leads to better analysis and better decision-making.

In the overall market, Intrum expects better visibility going forward. With a lower cost of capital, NPL portfolios will become more attractive.

Market growth is expected across both Servicing and Investing, driven by higher expected loan volumes, a stabilisation in the cost of capital and a normalisation of pricing expectations.

Management made it clear that AI is not a central key for their strategy, but it is an optional upside and an opportunity if executed correctly.

The strategy also sets the direction for the continued rollout of Ophelos, transitioning from local market customisation and integration to a scalable, standalone AI product and operating model with clear specifications to support sustainable growth, with Portugal serving as the initial blueprint.

Intrum plans to grow their investing for NPLs as soon as the market becomes more investable and how fast Intrum will be able to leverage their ratio. Management states that this could take place between 2026 and 2030. However, improved credit ratings will also be a key factor.

Valuation

I have made three different valuation scenarios. A bear case, a base case, and a bull case. Each case has different metrics, and they showcase the potential upside of each scenario.

Intrum has a huge discount and has a P/S of 0.3x. It is priced for disaster, which is justified considering they have negative growth while having solvency problems. However, if they manage to get past the worst, there is a big opportunity here, as shown below.

Risk

Intrum is a heavily distressed company, and that shows in their valuation, with P/S approximately 0.3 and EBITDA below market cap.

Metric Value (FY 2025) Analysis
Net Debt SEK 44.1 Bn

Down from SEK 49.9 Bn in 2024. Improvement is due to asset sales and debt restructuring, not organic cash generation.
Leverage Ratio 4.8x

Management targets 3.5x by 2026. 4.8x is still very high for a company with shrinking investment volumes.
Equity Ratio 19.5%

Total Equity (12.8 Bn) / Total Assets (65.5 Bn). A thin buffer against further asset write-downs.
Interest Coverage 2.6x EBITDA (9.1 Bn) / Net Financial Expenses (3.5 Bn). Most operational profit goes to paying lenders.

Below is a full schedule of loan maturity, and 2027 will be a year of high pressure, and investors will be following closely about the financial performance, as servicing growth will be an important driver for higher financial stability, as investing volumes will most likely continue to fall into 2026.

Debt Instrument Currency Nominal Amount (M) Nominal Amount (SEK M) Maturity Date
Term Loan EUR 25 271 30/06/2026
Term Loan EUR 25 271 31/03/2027
Exchange Notes EUR 332 3,595 11/09/2027
Exchange Notes SEK 418 418 11/09/2027
New Money Notes EUR 511 5,530 11/09/2027
New Money Notes SEK 173 173 11/09/2027
RCF (Revolving Credit) EUR 1,074 11,620 30/06/2028
Exchange Notes EUR 614 6,642 11/09/2028
Exchange Notes SEK 795 795 11/09/2028
Exchange Notes EUR 589 6,374 11/09/2029
Exchange Notes SEK 795 795 11/09/2029
Exchange Notes EUR 737 7, bvw 11/09/2030
Exchange Notes SEK 953 953 11/09/2030
TOTAL 45,407

Looking at cash flows, 2025 was not a bad year. Net operating income was negative for the quarter, due to huge non-cash effects. FY 2025 net cash flow from operating activities grew by around 5% YoY and reached 8.5 billion SEK. During 2027, the critical event of maturity is the 11th of September, when approximately SEK 9.7 billion of debt matures. They have to either continue selling assets or grow so they can be able to pay down the debt. The reality is that they are now under-investing to keep cash flows high, and that pressures growth as their investing segment continues to decline.

Conclusion

Intrum's current situation is a lot, and this investment is not for the risk-averse. They have laid out a clear growth strategy going forward, and I believe there is much potential in this industry. The overall industry has about 10% automated operations, so the potential for efficiency gains here is huge. For they continue to improve margins and cut costs, and this is impressive. The rate of change in growth has turned positive for Intrum, and the past two quarters had positive organic growth in servicing, which are good signs. However, due to their big solvency issues and uncertainties, I cannot give them a BUY rating. Therefore, I rate Intrum a HOLD!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
